findings.claim: This diff flips the static default of `disable_cascade_attn` from `False` to `True` in ModelConfig (vllm/config/model.py:220). Cascade attention is a throughput optimization for the V1 engine: when many requests share a long common prefix, the shared-prefix attention is computed once instead of every request re-reading the shared prefix KV (memory-bandwidth bound). The runtime consumes this default directly: `gpu_model_runner.py:450` sets `self.cascade_attn_enabled = not self.model_config.disable_cascade_attn`, and line 3627 gates cascade use on `self.cascade_attn_enabled`. With the new default True, cascade attention is now globally OFF unless users explicitly opt in via `--disable-cascade-attn=False`. For high-concurrency shared-prefix workloads (e.g. common system prompts, few-shot prefixes), this forces the slower non-cascade path by default, matching the config-toggle-perf-feature prior (static config default choosing the slow path). Note the change is intentional (opt-in to avoid numerical issues), but it constitutes a default throughput regression for prefix-sharing workloads.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput benchmark with many concurrent requests sharing a long common prefix (e.g. shared long system prompt / few-shot context), comparing disable_cascade_attn=False vs True, measuring tokens/sec and prefill KV read bandwidth.

This diff is itself the FIX for a suboptimal-kernel-gate regression, not an introduction of one. At the parent commit, the caller in fused_moe.py passed `naive_block_assignment` positionally as the 8th argument to `self.punica_wrapper.moe_lora_align_block_size(...)`. But the actual runtime dispatch target is PunicaWrapperGPU.moe_lora_align_block_size, whose 8th positional parameter is `expert_map` (not `naive_block_assignment`). Because the call also passed `expert_map` positionally just before it, the argument ordering was misaligned: the computed `naive_block_assignment` value was landing on the wrong parameter (`pad_sorted_ids`) while `naive_block_assignment` itself stayed at its default `False`. Net effect: the fast/naive block-assignment path (which skips allocating sorted_ids/expert_ids buffers and the ops.moe_lora_align_block_size kernel launch when experts*loras dominate) was never taken — the gate always rejected the fast kernel. The fix (`naive_block_assignment=naive_block_assignment` keyword, plus adding the param to the base class signature) correctly enables the fast path. So the change RESTORES the optimization rather than regressing it.

findings.claim: Flips the default of --data-parallel-sharding-strategy from 'no_shard' to 'optim_grads_params' (ZeRO-3 full parameter+gradient+optimizer sharding for Megatron-FSDP). Per the repo's own README (fsdp/src/README.md:287), 'no_shard' is the least memory-efficient but MOST communication-efficient strategy, while 'optim_grads_params' is the most memory-efficient but LEAST communication-efficient — it requires all-gathering full parameter shards before each forward/backward layer compute and reduce-scatter of gradients every bprop step. Changing the default silently opts every FSDP job that does not explicitly set this flag into per-layer all-gather/reduce-scatter communication that was previously absent (no_shard = replicated params, single grad all-reduce). If comm/compute overlap is imperfect this materializes as extra per-iteration collective latency and potential compute bubbles. This is a static default-value change that routes users onto the higher-comm-overhead path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-iteration step time and DP-collective volume between --data-parallel-sharding-strategy=no_shard and =optim_grads_params on a fixed FSDP model/DP-size, measuring all-gather/reduce-scatter time and overlap efficiency.

This PR gates the EP weight-filter (skip-non-local-expert-weights) loading optimization behind a new `enable_ep_weight_filter` toggle that DEFAULTS to False, thereby disabling by default an I/O optimization that was previously always-on. In the parent, `_init_ep_weight_filter` activated whenever `model_config.is_moe and parallel_config.enable_expert_parallel` (introduced by prior perf commit 821eb80c0 'Skip non-local expert weights during EP model loading'). It computed `local_expert_ids` and threaded them into `safetensors_weights_iterator(..., local_expert_ids=...)`, so each rank read only its own expert shard from disk. With this diff, the guard adds `and parallel_config.enable_ep_weight_filter`, which is `False` by default, so `_init_ep_weight_filter` now early-returns and `self.local_expert_ids` stays None. Result: every MoE+EP run reads ALL expert weights from disk on every rank again (N-fold storage I/O for per-expert-tensor checkpoints like DeepSeek/Mixtral/Kimi), i.e. slow-path-by-default. This is a static default value choosing the slow path, not a runtime predicate misfire.

As shipped, no existing deployment gets the optimization unless config is changed.

In StructuredOutputsWorker.apply_grammar_bitmask (hot decode-time path when structured outputs are active), the refactor replaced the preallocated pinned-buffer design (UvaBufferPool, which reused fixed pinned host buffers via slice assignment) with per-call fresh pinned-host allocations. The bitmask copy now goes through async_copy_to_gpu, whose body does `tmp = x.pin_memory()` — allocating a NEW pinned host buffer for the full (num_masks, cdiv(vocab_size,32)) array on every step. Allocating pinned host memory (cudaHostAlloc/register) is expensive and synchronizing, and re-doing it each call is wasted work versus the old preallocated buffer. This is exactly the redundant per-call host-buffer allocation/copy the original code's NOTE was designed to avoid ("to save a unnecessary CPU-to-CPU copy").

The diff adds a JIT C++ extension (torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline) to replace the previous UntypedStorage.copy_. _get_share_storage() lazily compiles this extension on first call to set_output_tensor(). load_inline invokes a full nvcc/g++ compile+link cycle (tens of seconds to minutes on the first activation-recompute backward), blocking the training step. If the build/lock directory (TORCH_EXTENSIONS_DIR / ~/.cache/torch_extensions) is not persistent or is shared across many ranks (multi-GPU/multi-node), every process races to compile the same extension, causing file-lock contention and repeated recompiles. This is a severe first-iteration latency / warmup regression on a path that previously did zero compilation.

The default_moe_runner.py change actually REMOVES a per-layer .clone() of hidden_states/shared_input on the shared-experts dual-stream path. Instead of cloning before switching streams, it now records the original shared_experts_input on the shared_experts_stream and passes it directly. This eliminates an allocation + copy of the full [tokens, hidden_dim] tensor on every MoE forward — a positive change, not a regression. The historical shared-mutable-state-contention / race case is guarded here: the code adds `assert self.moe_config.disable_inplace`, meaning the routed-expert kernel will NOT mutate the shared input in place (layer.py already sets disable_inplace=True when shared_experts is not None), so removing the clone is safe and does not re-introduce the earlier race where the routed kernel mutated hidden_states concurrently. findings.claim: The receiver (P1) cache in `BaseMultiModalReceiverCache.get_and_update_features` is re-keyed from `feature.identifier` (LoRA-prefixed, e.g. `<lora_name>:<mm_hash>`) to `feature.mm_hash or feature.identifier` (base mm_hash without LoRA prefix). However, the P0 sender-side cache (`MultiModalProcessorSenderCache` / `ShmObjectStoreSenderCache`) and the `is_cached`/`get_and_update_item` path in `processing.py` still key on the full (LoRA-aware) hash. The whole mirrored-cache design in `cache.py` requires that P0 and P1 caches evict/hit on IDENTICAL keys ('keys in P0 and P1 caches are mirrored, allowing us to determine whether a key is cached in P1 by looking up the P0 cache, without having to communicate with P1'). With the receiver now keyed on base mm_hash but the sender keyed on LoRA-prefixed hash, P0 may believe an item is NOT cached (different key per LoRA) and re-serialize/IPC-transfer the large MultiModalKwargs, while P1 already has it — or vice versa, P0 believes it is cached and sets mm_input=None while P1's base-mm_hash entry got evicted. This is the exact `ipc-transport-overhead` mechanism: mismatched cache membership causes redundant serialization/IPC of pixel-value tensors.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run vision LoRA with mm_processor_cache_gb>0 and IPC cache enabled (single API proc), send repeated identical images across two LoRAs, and measure P0->P1 IPC bytes / MultiModalKwargs serialization count vs a baseline that keys P1 by identifier.

The main sampling hot path (`Sampler.sample` -> `gumbel_sample` -> `_gumbel_sample_kernel`) is converted to run in FP64. The diff adds `logits = logits.to(tl.float64)`, so the per-block `[num_tokens, vocab_size]` logits (BLOCK_SIZE=1024 per program instance, num_blocks = ceil(vocab_size/1024)) reduction (`tl.max`), the `logits + gumbel_noise` add, and the double-log noise transform all execute in FP64. On most NVIDIA GPUs used in serving (H100 SXM, L40S, RTX/consumer, and Ada/Ampere consumer parts) the SIMT FP64 throughput is 1/32 to 1/64 of FP32, so this vocab-wide elementwise + reduction pass over the logits becomes 32-64x slower in ALU-bound regions and is executed on every decode step for every request. This is a per-step, per-token cost in the critical decode loop.

This doubles the coordinator fan-out over all single_type_managers for every candidate request scheduled each step. Cost is O(num_kv_cache_groups) per request per schedule() and is pure overhead when the request would have fit anyway (the common steady-state case).

findings.claim: In NixlConnector.prefer_cross_layer_blocks, the enable flag is parsed as `bool(str(extra_config.get("enable_cross_layers_blocks", "False")))`. Because `bool(str(x))` is truthy for any non-empty string (including the string "False"), this predicate returns True whenever the value is a string like "False" (the default) — the guard misfires and the fast/legacy path (non-cross-layer layout) is unintentionally skipped. On FLASH_ATTN/FLASHINFER backends this forces use_uniform_kv_cache()/allocate_uniform_kv_caches() to build the cross-layer contiguous KV layout by default even when the user did NOT opt in. This silently changes the KV cache memory layout for all NIXL P/D deployments on those backends, altering physical block placement and NIXL region registration/transfer behavior for every request — a broad performance/behavior regression enabled-by-default rather than gated behind the documented opt-in flag. Correct parsing should be e.g. `str(...).lower() == 'true'` (mirrors run_accuracy_test.sh which passes the string "True").
findings.suggested_benchmark: P/D disaggregation NIXL transfer benchmark on FLASH_ATTN backend, comparing KV cache registration time and per-request transfer throughput with enable_cross_layers_blocks unset (default) before vs after this change.

findings.claim: This diff inserts a bidirectional stream barrier (`self.reduction_stream.wait_stream(current_stream())` + `current_stream().wait_stream(self.reduction_stream)`) in ipg_remove_grads before the ping-pong ipg_buffer index swap, guarded by `if not get_accelerator().resolves_data_dependency()`. This is a correctness fix for the ZeRO-2 double-buffer swap race (loss->0 at step 3, issue #7188): the reduction stream must finish consuming buffer[index] before it is reused via the 1-index swap. The bidirectional sync serializes the reduction (comm) stream and the default/compute stream at the bucket boundary, adding a GPU-side synchronization/bubble that reduces the compute-communication overlap the overlap_comm path exists to provide. This matches the historical correctness-forces-slow-path pattern (symptom: gpu-util-or-bubble). It is intentional and required for correctness, and it is properly gated by resolves_data_dependency() so accelerators that implicitly resolve data dependencies (e.g. via CUDA graph stream capture semantics) skip it; the pre-swap sync also only runs at bucket-full boundaries (contiguous_gradients and overlap_comm), not per-parameter, so the overhead is bounded. Not removable without reintroducing the gradient-corruption bug.

The rewritten HybridKVCacheCoordinator.find_longest_cache_hit replaces the original single-pass algorithm (full attention once, then 'other' once bounded by full's hit) with an iterative fixed-point `while True` loop that restarts checks over ALL attention groups whenever any group reduces the hit length. Crucially, only full-attention groups get the cheap 'reuse cached blocks' fast-path (`is_full_attn and cached_blocks is not None`); every non-full group (SlidingWindow, Mamba, differing-window SlidingWindow) re-runs manager_cls.find_longest_cache_hit() from scratch on every outer iteration. Each such call is an O(max_num_blocks) scan doing block_pool.get_cached_block() lookups over the block-hash chain. With G non-full groups where each successive group shrinks the candidate length, the loop can require up to O(G) restarts, each re-scanning all G non-full groups => O(G^2) full rescans of the block-hash chain per request, versus O(1) passes in the original two-type implementation. find_longest_cache_hit is on the per-request prefix-cache lookup hot path (get_computed_blocks), so for hybrid models with 3-4+ groups and long prompts this is a real per-request throughput regression.

D2H staging semantics changed: previously `_copy_to_cpu` did an eager non-blocking `ten.to('cpu', non_blocking=True)` at plan/prepare time (overlapping the copy with subsequent compute), followed by `torch.cuda.synchronize()` right before forking. The diff replaces `_copy_to_cpu` with `_clone_if_needed`, which for GPU tensors now returns the tensor unchanged and DEFERS the D2H copy to `preload_fn`/`preload_tensors`, invoked at schedule time. In the PersistentAsyncCaller path the D2H copy runs inside `schedule_async_call` and the trainer blocks on `self.preload_q.join()` before proceeding. This serializes the entire GPU->host copy on the training critical path (the trainer waits for the worker to finish preloading), removing the compute/copy overlap that the original eager non_blocking copy provided. On memory-intensive/large-model runs this can add measurable stall to every checkpoint step.

findings.claim: In `check_availability`, the admission gate is restructured: `request_can_be_added` now requires `self.total_request_count < self.max_requests AND self.paused_request_count == 0`, whereas the token gate `request_tokens_can_be_added` no longer carries the `paused_request_count == 0` condition. Net effect is that no brand-new request can be admitted while ANY request is paused (paused requests are those evicted under KV-cache memory pressure). This is exactly the historical batch-composition-mismatch mechanism: while requests are paused, admission of new requests is blocked, starving the active batch and increasing tail latency / lowering effective concurrency even when token/block budget could accommodate new work.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure request admission latency and GPU batch occupancy in a mixed workload that induces frequent pausing (long sequences + bursty arrivals); compare time-to-first-token for new requests when paused_request_count > 0 before vs after this change.

In the non-FP8 dense decode path, the diff removes the persistent-buffer copy scheme for the FlashMLA scheduler metadata. Previously `_build_decode` copied `tile_scheduler_metadata`/`num_splits` into the pre-allocated persistent buffers (`cg_buf_tile_scheduler_metadata`/`cg_buf_num_splits`) when `cudagraph_mode.has_full_cudagraphs()`, guaranteeing stable device addresses for CUDA-graph replay. After the change, for the non-FP8 case `_build_decode` just passes the freshly-allocated `scheduler_metadata` object straight into `FlashMLADecodeMetadata` with no copy into the persistent buffer. Each rebuild allocates a new tensor at a fresh address, so a captured full CUDA graph will keep reading the stale capture-time metadata pointer while the new metadata lives elsewhere — the exact 'static-buffer/pointer identity broken under full cudagraph' mechanism from the historical case. Only the `is_fp8_kvcache` branch still writes into buffers, and even there it assigns raw kernel outputs (`scheduler_metadata.tile_scheduler_metadata = tile_scheduler_metadata`) rather than copying into a persistent buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable full CUDA graphs (compilation_config.cudagraph_mode with full cudagraphs) on a Hopper MLA decode workload (e.g. DeepSeek) with bf16 KV cache and verify decode correctness/throughput vs eager; specifically vary batch size across replays to expose stale scheduler-metadata pointers.

findings.claim: The FlashMLASparse FP8 path (`_build_fp8_mixed_decode_prefill` and `_build_fp8_separate_prefill_decode`) also drops the copy into the persistent `tile_scheduler_metadata_buffer`/`num_splits_buffer`. Previously it did `tile_scheduler_metadata_buffer.copy_(...)` / `num_splits_view.copy_(...)` and stored the buffer views into `FP8KernelMetadata`; now it directly stores the fresh `scheduler_metadata` object returned by `get_mla_metadata`. Under full CUDA graph capture (builder declares `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_BATCH`) this breaks the fixed-address assumption that the persistent buffers were introduced to satisfy, risking stale-pointer reads on replay. The persistent buffers (`self.tile_scheduler_metadata_buffer`, `self.num_splits_buffer`) are now allocated but unused for these paths.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run FlashMLA sparse FP8 decode (fp8_ds_mla KV cache, DeepSeek-V3.2 style) under full CUDA graph mode across differing decode batch sizes and confirm output stability across graph replays.

The refactor moves output-type validation from a single post-hoc call `engine_class.validate_outputs(outputs, ...)` (which was actually a no-op returning `outputs` unchanged) into a per-output `assert isinstance(output, output_type)` inside the hot loop of `_run_engine` (`for output in step_outputs: assert isinstance(...)`). This adds an isinstance check for every output produced on every engine step. Previously `validate_outputs` performed zero work (it just returned the list); now there is a per-item type check executed in the tightest offline decode loop. The cost is small (one isinstance per finished/unfinished step output) but it is genuinely new per-iteration work versus the removed no-op, and it runs even in production (asserts are only skipped under `python -O`). For high-throughput offline batches with many outputs per step this is a measurable, if minor, added overhead. Consider validating once per batch or gating behind a debug flag rather than asserting on every step output.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Offline throughput benchmark (benchmarks/benchmark_throughput.py) with a large batch of short prompts to maximize outputs-per-step; compare tokens/s before vs after, and also with `python -O` to isolate the assert cost.

`EngineCoreOutput` is a `msgspec.Struct` serialized/deserialized over ZMQ between engine core and frontend for every output. The new `prefill_stats: PrefillStats | None` field points to a plain `@dataclass` (vllm/v1/metrics/stats.py PrefillStats), NOT a msgspec.Struct. msgspec/msgpack cannot natively encode an arbitrary dataclass, so when prefill_stats is non-None (i.e. on the first/prefill output of every request) it falls into MsgpackEncoder.enc_hook, which — unless VLLM_ALLOW_INSECURE_SERIALIZATION is set — raises TypeError ('Object of type ... is not serializable'). If that env is set, it instead falls back to pickle.dumps() per prefill output. Either a hard break or a per-request pickle round-trip that the previous two plain int fields (num_cached_tokens / num_external_computed_tokens) never incurred. The replaced fields were bare ints trivially encodable by msgspec; PrefillStats is not. Recommend making PrefillStats a msgspec.Struct (like EngineCoreEvent) so it stays on the fast native codec path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Encode/decode an EngineCoreOutput carrying a non-None PrefillStats via MsgpackEncoder/MsgpackDecoder; compare to parent (two int fields) for latency and correctness under both VLLM_ALLOW_INSECURE_SERIALIZATION on/off.
